title: Your Biggest Supplier Doubles Prices Overnight
setup: Your largest supplier — responsible for 40% of your inventory — just informed you that prices are increasing 100% effective next month, citing raw material shortages. You have 30 days before your next order is due.
questions: Do you absorb the cost, pass it to customers, or split the difference?
What would it take to qualify a backup supplier in 30 days?
Does this change your pricing strategy for the rest of the year?

title: Your #1 Competitor Just Went Free
setup: A well-funded competitor announced they're making their core product free, funded by a new round of venture capital. Your product is priced at $99/month and has similar features.
questions: What in your product is defensible against a free competitor?
Do you compete on price, or double down on a different value prop?
How do you communicate this to your sales team this week?

title: A Key Machine Fails With No Replacement Parts
setup: The machine responsible for 60% of your production output has failed. The manufacturer discontinued the part five years ago, and the earliest custom-fabricated replacement is 8 weeks out.
questions: Can you shift volume to other lines or a subcontractor in the interim?
Which customer commitments are at risk, and who do you call first?
Should this failure change your capital equipment strategy going forward?

title: A New Regulation Bans Your Core Billing Practice
setup: A new regulation takes effect in 90 days that prohibits the billing method your practice has relied on for 70% of revenue. Competitors face the same rule.
questions: What alternative revenue models are viable within the compliance window?
Is this a threat, or an opportunity to differentiate faster than competitors adapt?
Who internally owns compliance risk, and are they resourced for it?

title: A Viral Review Tanks Your Booking Rate
setup: A widely-shared negative review — factually exaggerated but not entirely false — has cut new bookings by 35% in one week. Local press has picked up the story.
questions: What's your first public response, and who approves it?
How do you rebuild trust with guests who already have reservations?
What operational gap did the review expose that's worth fixing regardless of PR impact?

title: Your Top Biller Gives Two Weeks' Notice
setup: Your highest-billing consultant, responsible for 25% of firm revenue and several key client relationships, just resigned with the minimum notice period to join a competitor.
questions: Which clients are at flight risk, and who reaches out first?
Is this a signal of a deeper retention problem, or an isolated case?
What should change about how the firm concentrates relationship ownership going forward?
